title: Automate SAP order-to-cash with multi-agent AI & Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)
summary: Google demonstrated how to automate SAP order-to-cash processes using the Agent Development Kit and Model Context Protocol to replace brittle RPA with resilient multi-agent teams. The approach uses Gemini's context window and parallel execution to handle complex enterprise workflows through APIs.
writeup: This video demonstrates automating SAP's order-to-cash process using Google ADK and the Model Context Protocol, replacing brittle RPA bots with a structured multi-agent team that calls SAP OData APIs directly as MCP tools. The core insight is that ADK's hierarchical agent architecture mirrors corporate org charts: a supervisor root agent delegates to specialized sub-agents (a sales order creator and an inventory reviewer), each scoped to specific tools. A working demo creates a real sales order on a live SAP system, with the agent team reasoning through parameters, calling tools in parallel (a feature added in ADK 1.1), and returning a final summary including the calculated total of $208,000.

key_points: SAP's OData API specifications can be fed into the OpenAPI MCP generator CLI to automatically produce a full MCP server and tool set, sidestepping the need to hand-code hundreds of API integrations for large modules like Sales and Distribution or Material Management.

RPA (robotic process automation) is brittle because it mimics screen interaction and breaks on UI or resolution changes; the API-plus-MCP approach is more robust since it calls stable programmatic interfaces directly.

ADK agents can be nested hierarchically so a supervisor root agent holds no MCP tools itself but delegates to sub-agents that each own a scoped tool set, allowing agent team design to mirror existing organizational roles and responsibilities without redesigning business processes.
Gemini's 1-million-token context window is a practical necessity at SAP scale, where a single module can expose hundreds of APIs that would otherwise overflow a smaller context.
As of ADK version 1.1, agents can invoke multiple MCP tools in parallel, which the demo exploits when adding two separate line items to a sales order in a single pass.

The demo agent does more than parameter-passing: it constructs OData filter expressions autonomously to retrieve only the fields needed, demonstrating genuine reasoning rather than rote tool invocation.
